title: Derivatives by the Chain Rule - MIT OpenCourseWare
relevance: Provides a clear derivation of the chain rule and multiple examples of polynomial‑inside‑trig and trig‑inside‑exponential compositions.

heading: Lesson 3.1 – Chain Rule and Composite Functions
body: A composite function f(g(x)) is read as “f of g of x.”  Using limits we derive the chain rule: (f∘g)'(x)=f'(g(x))·g'(x).  Examples include h(x)=sin(x^2) and k(x)=e^{3x}.  A table of common inner‑outer pairs (polynomial‑trig, trig‑exp, log‑power, etc.) helps students recognize patterns before differentiating.

title: Visualizing Composite Functions
caption: Plot f(g(x)) for several inner‑outer pairs (e.g., sin(x^2), e^{3x}, ln(√x)) and observe how the slope changes with the chain rule.

questions.explanation: Outer derivative e^{u}=e^{u}, inner u=3x^2, u'=6x, so k'=6x·e^{3x^2}.

title: Implicit Differentiation and Related Rates – RIT Handout
relevance: Explains step‑by‑step implicit differentiation, isolates dy/dx, and includes related‑rates examples such as expanding circles.

heading: Lesson 3.2 – Implicit Differentiation and Related Rates
body: Some curves, like x²+y²=r², cannot be solved explicitly for y.  By treating y as an implicit function of x and differentiating both sides, we isolate dy/dx.  The same technique combines with the chain rule for related‑rates problems, e.g., a circle whose radius grows at a known speed.

title: Implicit Circle and Hyperbola
caption: Graph x²+y²=9 and xy=1.  Use the slider for r in x²+y²=r² and watch how dy/dx changes.

questions.q: For x² + y² = 25, what is dy/dx?
A circle’s radius increases at 2 m/s.  At r=3 m, what is the rate of change of its area?

title: Derivatives of Exponential, Logarithmic and Trigonometric Functions – Lia Vas
relevance: Lists derivative formulas for sin, cos, tan, e^x, ln x, and inverse trig functions, with proofs that use limits and the chain rule.

heading: Lesson 3.3 – Derivatives of Trig, Exponential, and Logarithmic Functions
body: Using the limit definitions we derive d/dx(sin x)=cos x, d/dx(e^x)=e^x, and d/dx(ln x)=1/x.  Inverse trig derivatives require domain restrictions (e.g., arcsin′(x)=1/√(1−x²) for |x|<1).  Composite examples such as e^{sin x} combine these rules.

title: e^{sin x} and Other Mixes
caption: Plot y = e^{sin x} and its derivative y' = e^{sin x}·cos x.  Observe how the slope follows the product of the outer and inner derivatives.

questions.explanation: Outer derivative cos(u) with u=e^x, then multiply by u'=e^x.
Apply chain rule: outer e^{u} → e^{u}, inner u=sin x → cos x.

title: Higher‑Order Derivatives, Velocity, and Acceleration – TDChristian
relevance: Defines second, third (jerk), and higher derivatives, connects them to motion concepts, and includes projectile‑motion examples.

heading: Lesson 3.4 – Higher‑Order Derivatives and Motion Problems
body: The second derivative gives acceleration; the third derivative (jerk) measures how acceleration changes.  By successive differentiation we can model projectile motion, s(t)=v₀t−½gt², and more complex systems such as a damped harmonic oscillator governed by x''+2ζωx'+ω²x=0.

title: Projectile Motion and Jerk
caption: Plot position s(t)=t³−6t²+9t, velocity v(t)=s'(t), acceleration a(t)=v'(t), and jerk j(t)=a'(t).  Observe how each derivative relates to motion.

questions.explanation: v(t)=3t²−12t+9; v(2)=3·4−24+9=12−24+9=−3? Wait compute: 12−24+9=−3. Correct answer is -3 (option 1).

questions.q: A projectile launched upward with v₀=20 m/s.  Maximum height occurs when:
questions.explanation: At the apex the velocity is zero.

questions.q: If acceleration a(t)=6t−4, what is the jerk?
questions.explanation: Jerk is the derivative of acceleration: a'(t)=6.

title: Chain Rule Tutorial
caption: A concise video walkthrough of the chain rule with multiple practice problems.

title: Ask the Tutor
persona: You are a calculus tutor for differentiation techniques. Be concise, ask guiding questions, and encourage the learner to reason step‑by‑step.
greeting: Hi! Ask me anything about chain rule, implicit differentiation, trig/exponential/log derivatives, or higher‑order motion problems.
